name: Narfi
description: Narfi, also known as Nörfi or Nari, is a figure shrouded in tragedy within Norse mythology, primarily known for his horrific fate. He is the son of Loki and Sigyn, and brother to Váli (sometimes referred to as a son of Loki, though accounts vary). Narfi's story is intrinsically linked to the punishment of Loki for his role in the death of Baldr. Following Baldr's death, the Æsir hunted down Loki and his children. In a particularly gruesome act of vengeance, Váli was transformed into a wolf, and in his wolfish form, he tore his brother Narfi to pieces. Narfi's entrails were then used to bind Loki to a rock, where Skadi placed a venomous serpent above him, its venom dripping onto his face. Sigyn remained by Loki's side, catching the venom in a bowl, but whenever she had to empty it, the venom would drip onto Loki, causing him to writhe in agony, resulting in earthquakes. Narfi's death is a stark reminder of the brutal justice and internecine conflict that characterizes much of Norse mythology, especially in the events leading up to Ragnarök.
subtitle: Victim of Vengeance

keyMyths.title: The Binding of Loki
keyMyths.description: Following Baldr's death and Loki's subsequent capture, the Æsir sought a fitting punishment for the trickster god. They transformed Váli into a wolf, and in his bestial state, Váli killed his brother Narfi. The Æsir then used Narfi's entrails, now hardened into iron-like bonds, to restrain Loki to a rock within a cave. This act highlights the brutal and familial nature of Norse justice, where even brothers could be turned against each other to exact revenge. This binding was a crucial event leading up to Ragnarök.

keyMyths.title: The Torment of Loki
keyMyths.description: After being bound with Narfi's entrails, Loki was subjected to further torment. Skadi, as recompense for the death of her father Thjazi, placed a venomous serpent above Loki's head. The serpent's venom dripped constantly onto Loki's face, causing him immense pain. Sigyn, Loki's loyal wife, remained by his side, catching the venom in a bowl. However, when she had to empty the bowl, the venom would drip onto Loki, causing him to writhe in agony, resulting in earthquakes. This symbolizes the unending suffering that awaits those who defy the gods.

keyMyths.title: Váli's Transformation
keyMyths.description: The transformation of Váli into a wolf to kill his own brother, Narfi, is a particularly disturbing aspect of this myth. This act underscores the power and ruthlessness of the Æsir, who were willing to manipulate even familial bonds to achieve their goals. The transformation itself is a testament to the magical abilities possessed by the gods and their willingness to use them to inflict suffering and maintain order. The act also raises questions about Váli's own agency in the event.

sources.author: Snorri Sturluson
sources.description: A primary source for Norse mythology, providing detailed accounts of the gods, creation, and Ragnarök. Contains the most extensive account of Narfi's death and Loki's binding.
sources.text: A primary source for Norse mythology, providing detailed accounts of the gods, creation, and Ragnarök. Contains the most extensive account of Narfi's death and Loki's binding.

sources.author: Rudolf Simek
sources.description: A comprehensive dictionary providing scholarly analysis of Norse myths, figures, and concepts. Useful for understanding the etymology and variations of Narfi's name.
sources.text: A comprehensive dictionary providing scholarly analysis of Norse myths, figures, and concepts. Useful for understanding the etymology and variations of Narfi's name.

sources.author: Hilda Ellis Davidson
sources.work: Gods and Myths of Northern Europe
sources.description: A detailed overview of Norse mythology, exploring the social and cultural contexts of the myths. Provides insight into the themes of revenge and punishment in Norse society.
sources.text: A detailed overview of Norse mythology, exploring the social and cultural contexts of the myths. Provides insight into the themes of revenge and punishment in Norse society.
